sections.title: 比 (bǐ) — More Than
sections.explanation: The most common way to make comparisons in Chinese is with 比 (bǐ). The basic structure is A 比 B + adjective: '他比我高' (He is taller than me). Unlike English, you do NOT add a separate word like 'more' — the adjective alone carries the comparative meaning after 比. You can also specify the exact degree of difference by adding an amount after the adjective: '我比他高五厘米' (I am 5 cm taller than him), '她比我大三岁' (She is 3 years older than me). Important: do NOT use 很 (hěn) before the adjective in a 比 sentence. '他比我很高' is WRONG — just say '他比我高'.
sections.tip: Think of 比 as setting up a comparison direction: A 比 B = 'A compared-to B'. Whatever adjective follows tells you how A exceeds B. To add a specific amount, just tack it on after the adjective — no extra words needed.

examples.en: I am 5 cm taller than him.
She is 3 years older than me.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'He is taller than me' in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: The correct structure is A 比 B + adj: '他比我高'. Do not use 很 in a 比 comparison.

exercises.prompt: 今天___昨天热。(Today is hotter than yesterday.)
exercises.hint: Use the comparison word that goes between A and B.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I am 2 years younger than her'?
exercises.explanation: The specific amount (两岁) goes after the adjective (小): A 比 B + adj + amount.

sections.title: 没有...那么... — Not as...as
sections.explanation: To say 'A is not as (adjective) as B', use the structure A 没有 B 那么 + adjective: '我没有他那么高' (I am not as tall as him). This is the standard way to express negative comparisons. 那么 (nàme) can sometimes be omitted in casual speech, giving 'A 没有 B + adj': '我没有他高' also works. You can also use 这么 (zhème, 'this') instead of 那么 when referring to something nearby or just mentioned. This pattern is the natural negative counterpart to 比 — while 比 says A exceeds B, 没有...那么 says A does NOT reach B's level.
sections.tip: 没有...那么 is like saying 'doesn't reach that level of...'. It's the go-to way to soften a comparison — instead of saying someone is shorter, you say they're 'not as tall as'. This is considered more polite in Chinese.

examples.en: I am not as tall as him.

examples.zh: 这本书没有那本书那么有趣。
examples.en: This book is not as interesting as that book.
Today is not as cold as yesterday.
He doesn't run as fast as me.

exercises.prompt: 弟弟___哥哥那么高。(The younger brother is not as tall as the older brother.)
exercises.hint: Use the negative comparison word meaning 'does not have / does not reach'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'This movie is not as good as that one'?
options: 这部电影比那部不好看。
这部电影没有那部那么好看。
这部电影不比那部好看那么。
exercises.explanation: A 没有 B 那么 + adj: '这部电影没有那部那么好看'. Don't negate 比 with 不 for 'not as...as'.

exercises.prompt: Which word can optionally replace 那么 in the pattern 'A 没有 B ___ adj'?
exercises.explanation: 这么 (zhème) can replace 那么 (nàme) when referring to something nearby. Both mean 'as...as' in this negative comparison structure.

sections.title: 跟...一样 — Same as
sections.explanation: To say 'A is the same as B', use A 跟 B 一样 (yīyàng): '我跟他一样高' (I am as tall as him). 跟 (gēn) means 'with' here and can be replaced by 和 (hé) in this pattern. When you add an adjective after 一样, it means 'equally (adjective)': '这个跟那个一样贵' (This one is as expensive as that one). To express difference, negate with 不: A 跟 B 不一样 means 'A and B are different / not the same'. You can be specific about what's different: '我跟他不一样高' (We are not the same height) or simply '我们不一样' (We are different).
sections.tip: 跟...一样 is your go-to for equality comparisons. Without an adjective it means 'the same'; with an adjective it means 'equally [adj]'. For 'different', just add 不 before 一样.

examples.en: I am as tall as him.
This one is as expensive as that one.
Chinese and Japanese are not the same.

exercises.prompt: 她___我一样大。(She is the same age as me.)
exercises.hint: Use the word meaning 'with' that starts the equality comparison pattern.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'This bag is as expensive as that one'?
options: 这个包比那个包一样贵。
这个包跟那个包一样贵。
这个包没有那个包一样贵。
这个包一样跟那个包贵。
exercises.explanation: A 跟 B 一样 + adj: '这个包跟那个包一样贵'. The adjective goes after 一样.

exercises.prompt: 我们的看法___。(Our views are different.)
exercises.hint: Negate 一样 with 不 to express 'different'.

sections.title: 越来越 and 越...越...
sections.explanation: 越来越 (yuè lái yuè) + adjective means 'more and more (adjective)' — it expresses a progressive change over time: '天气越来越冷' (The weather is getting colder and colder), '他的中文越来越好' (His Chinese is getting better and better). The pattern 越 A 越 B means 'the more A, the more B', where A and B can be verbs or adjectives: '越吃越胖' (The more you eat, the fatter you get), '越学越有意思' (The more you study, the more interesting it gets). In 越 A 越 B, the subject can appear before the whole phrase or between the two 越 clauses depending on what sounds natural.
sections.tip: 越来越 is for a single thing changing over time (more and more X). 越...越... links two things: the more one thing happens, the more another thing results. Both are extremely common in daily Chinese.

examples.en: The weather is getting colder and colder.
His Chinese is getting better and better.
The more you eat, the fatter you get.

examples.zh: 我越学中文越觉得有意思。
examples.en: The more I study Chinese, the more interesting I find it.

exercises.prompt: 她的汉语______好了。(Her Chinese is getting better and better.)
exercises.hint: Use the 'more and more' pattern with three characters.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'The more you practice, the better you get'?
exercises.explanation: 越 A 越 B: '越练习越好' — the more you practice (A), the better (B). The cause comes after the first 越.
越来越 + adj means 'more and more [adj]': 越来越贵 = more and more expensive / getting increasingly expensive.
